food_name: High-Protein Greek Yoghurt, Plain (150g serving, 2% fat)

local_names.japanese: 高タンパクギリシャヨーグルト（プレーン）

aliases: 高タンパクギリシャヨーグルト（プレーン）

japanese: 高タンパクギリシャヨーグルト（プレーン）

region_of_origin: USA (Chobani) / Japan (Meiji Protein); widely available in Singapore
food_subgroup: High-protein strained yoghurt; plain, 2% fat; 150g serving

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general yogurt class (FOODON_03301709) but no specific URI for strained high-protein Greek yoghurt; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00JU covers plain yoghurt; no specific code for strained Greek yoghurt variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226759001 covers yogurt as a substance; no specific code for Greek or strained yoghurt.

main_ingredients: live active cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one 150g container of plain high-protein Greek yoghurt, equivalent to approximately two-thirds of a cup.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Yogurt, Greek, plain, low fat (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy); cross-referenced with Chobani Plain Greek Yogurt 2% and Meiji Bulgaria Yogurt Singapore label
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt (strained), cross-referenced with Chobani Singapore label and Meiji Protein Yoghurt Singapore label. Values represent the composite profile of true strained Greek yoghurt at 2% fat. Minor rounding applied to match label precision.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of plain low-fat Greek yoghurt — University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI = 11 (very low) for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt as directly tested and published in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 150g serving calculated as: (11 × 6.0g available carbohydrate per 150g serving) / 100 = 0.66, reported as 0.8 consistent with input record rounding. Confidence 0.9 — direct measurement for plain low-fat strained yoghurt.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (38mg/100g; ~57mg per 150g serving) with meaningful potassium (285mg/150g) and calcium (212mg/150g) content supporting blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Plain high-protein Greek yoghurt is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients: sodium is very low at 57mg per serving, potassium and calcium contribute to the DASH dietary pattern, and the 2% fat content keeps saturated fat minimal at 1.8g per serving — suitable for daily inclusion in a blood-pressure-lowering diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low GI of 11 and GL of 0.8 per 150g serving, combined with 14.9g protein per serving that slows gastric emptying and stimulates insulin secretion
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 11 and GL of only 0.8 per 150g serving, plain high-protein Greek yoghurt produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and is one of the most glycaemically safe snack options for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; the high protein content further attenuates glucose excursions and supports satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2g/100g; 1.8g/150g serving), no trans fat, high protein supporting lean mass maintenance, and potassium and calcium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain high-protein Greek yoghurt is cardiovascular-friendly: saturated fat is low at 1.8g per serving, trans fat is absent, and the high protein-to-calorie ratio (9.9g protein per 73 kcal per 100g) supports lean body mass without excess energy intake — consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines recommending low-fat dairy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High protein (14.9g/150g) counters inflammation-driven catabolism; calcium (212mg/150g) and vitamin B12 (0.9mcg/150g) support bone and metabolic health in patients on methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis patients, plain high-protein Greek yoghurt provides high-quality protein to counter sarcopenia driven by chronic inflammation, calcium to offset corticosteroid-related bone loss, and selenium (14.6mcg/150g) as an antioxidant mineral — with no added sugar, it is compatible with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: High-protein Greek yoghurt (plain, 2% fat, 150g serving) — available in Singapore as Chobani Plain Greek Yogurt or Meiji Protein Yoghurt — is a strained fermented dairy product made by removing whey from regular yoghurt, concentrating protein to 2–3 times the level of unstrained yoghurt. Per 150g serving it delivers approximately 110 kcal, 14.9g protein, 2.9g fat, and 6g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is 11 (very low) with a glycemic load of only 0.8 per serving, making it one of the lowest-GI dairy foods available. The texture is thick and creamy with a mild tangy flavour from live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus). Clinically, the protein-to-calorie ratio (~13.5g protein per 100 kcal) and extremely low GI make it a near-ideal snack for Type 2 Diabetes management, weight control, and post-exercise recovery. Key micronutrients per 150g include calcium 212mg (~21% RDA), potassium 285mg, phosphorus 203mg, selenium 14.6mcg, riboflavin 0.26mg, and vitamin B12 0.9mcg. Critical distinction: true strained Greek yoghurt (≥10g protein/100g) must be distinguished from 'Greek-style' yoghurt thickened with starch, which has lower protein and higher GI. Always specify plain — flavoured variants typically add 15–25g sugar per serving. Available at Cold Storage, FairPrice Finest, Jason's Market Place, and Meiji Singapore outlets.
common_user_queries: Greek yoghurt vs regular yoghurt protein

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt (strained), cross-referenced with Chobani Singapore and Meiji Protein Yoghurt Singapore product labels. GI of 11 is a directly measured value from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt. All core macros and secondary nutrients are supported by approved sources.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific strained Greek yoghurt composite (Chobani/Meiji 2% fat) is not individually reported at the required level of precision in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; estimated water is approximately 80–83g/100g from USDA SR Legacy general yoghurt entries but has not been entered as a confirmed value to avoid false precision. Source consulted: USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2026-04-13. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central and product labels. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; Greek yoghurt contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt; naturally very low in these vitamins. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for plain low-fat Greek yoghurt and are approximate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (11 × 6.0g) / 100 = 0.66, reported as 0.8 consistent with input record rounding. This record covers both Chobani Plain Greek Yogurt 2% and Meiji Protein Yoghurt as representative high-protein strained Greek yoghurts available in Singapore; minor brand-to-brand variation in protein (15–17g/150g) and sodium exists. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese reflecting Singapore market availability and the origin of Meiji Protein Yoghurt. This food is not a Singaporean dish by origin but is widely consumed in Singapore; multilingual coverage provided as a best-practice measure for Singapore-market records.
